* InternVideo3 introduces **Multimodal Contextual Reasoning (MCR)**, framing long-video understanding as a closed-loop process of evidence accumulation and verification within a shared, evolving context.
* The architecture utilizes **Multimodal Multi-head Latent Attention (M^2LA)**, a token-preserving reparameterization designed to compress KV-cache states while maintaining the integrity of the full token stream.
* A **four-stage training pipeline** is employed, encompassing continued pretraining, short-to-long supervised fine-tuning, rule-based reinforcement learning, and on-policy distillation.
* The framework bridges the gap between text-dominant agents and **long-horizon multimodal tasks**, specifically improving performance in video-based iterative interaction and tool usage.
key_results: Achieved top-tier performance on Video-MME, MLVU, and EgoSchema benchmarks.
Introduced M^2LA to compress KV-cache states while retaining full token stream information.
Established a closed-loop reasoning process (MCR) for evidence-grounded behavior.
Demonstrated successful integration of retrieval tools for video-agentic tasks.
Validated a multi-stage training strategy ranging from pretraining to on-policy distillation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Shanghai AI Laboratory researchers introduce InternVideo3, a multimodal agent framework utilizing M^2LA for efficient, long-horizon video reasoning and interaction.

title: Agentic Environment Engineering for Large Language Models: A Survey of Environment Modeling, Synthesis, Evaluation, and Application
summary: Environments serve as interactive systems for large language model (LLM) based agents across diverse scenarios and play a crucial role in driving the continual evolution of model capabilities. Despite this importance, existing work lacks a systematic categorization and deep analysis. This paper systematically studies current researches on agentic environments from the perspective of the environment engineering lifecycle, covering their modeling, synthesis, evaluation and application. Specifically, the paper first introduces representative environments from the perspectives of eight attributes and eight domains, providing detailed analyses of their development paths and highlighting their core capabilities. Second, for automated environment synthesis, two paradigms are introduced, such as symbolic synthesis and neural synthesis. This paper also shows different environment evaluation methods in each paradigm. Thirdly, the corresponding environment applications from the perspective of agent-environment co-evolution are discussed. In specific, the paper characterizes the primary pathways for agent evolution in dynamic environments from four complementary perspectives: memory-centric experience evolution, orchestration-centric workflow evolution, trajectory-centric offline evolution, and exploration-centric online evolution. And three paradigms of environment evolution are identified, namely neural-driven, difficulty-driven, and scaling-driven approaches. At last, several promising future directions are discussed, including Environment-as-a-Service, Multi-agent Environments, and Neural-Symbolic Environments.

processed.summary:
* The paper formalizes the field of **Agentic Environment Engineering (AEE)**, providing a systematic taxonomy for modeling, synthesizing, and evaluating the interactive systems used to train and benchmark LLM agents.
* It distinguishes between **Symbolic Synthesis**, which uses structured logic and APIs to build environments, and **Neural Synthesis**, which utilizes generative models to simulate complex, high-fidelity worlds.
* The research outlines a framework for **Agent-Environment Co-evolution**, describing how agents improve through memory and trajectory optimization while environments evolve via scaling-driven and difficulty-driven paradigms.
key_results: Classification of environments across eight functional attributes including observability, determinism, and multi-agent support.
Identification of two major synthesis paradigms: symbolic-driven and neural-driven environment generation.
A four-pathway model for agent evolution: memory-centric, orchestration-centric, trajectory-centric, and exploration-centric.
Characterization of environment scaling through neural-driven, difficulty-driven, and data-scaling approaches.
Proposed vision for Environment-as-a-Service (EaaS) to standardize the deployment and benchmarking of agentic systems.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers provide a comprehensive framework for Agentic Environment Engineering to accelerate LLM agent development and evaluation co-evolution.

title: OpenMedReason: Scientific Reasoning Supervision for Medical Vision-Language Models
summary: High-stakes clinical use of large vision-language models (LVLMs) requires reasoning that is grounded in visual evidence and clinical knowledge, not just correct final answers. We introduce OpenMedReason, a large-scale, open multimodal medical reasoning corpus comprising approximately 450K image-question-answer instances whose reasoning traces are primarily derived from curated biomedical, human-authored scientific articles. OpenMedReason provides high-fidelity supervision beyond synthetic chains of thought, covering diverse medical domain vision modalities such as radiological scans, microscopic images, visible light photographs, charts, and others. We complement it with OpenMedReason-Bench, a held-out benchmark that allows fine-grained evaluation of LVLMs along three complementary axes of capability, including perception, medical knowledge, and rationale, enabling diagnostic evaluation beyond final-answer accuracy. OpenMedReason is a rich training resource that exhibits its effectiveness in both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement-based alignment. Training with OpenMedReason yields a 20% average improvement in VQA accuracy over the base model and achieves performance within 4.2% of the strongest comparable-scale medical LVLMs. Fine-grained performance analysis confirms that the gains are not concentrated in any single axis: OpenMedReason improves perception, medical knowledge, and rationale jointly, and its reasoning traces are preferred over those of the base model in 86.1% of pairwise comparisons. We release the code and dataset at huggingface.co/datasets/neginb/OpenMedReason.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **OpenMedReason**, a large-scale multimodal medical corpus of **450K instances** utilizing human-authored scientific reasoning traces for supervision.
- Establishes **OpenMedReason-Bench**, a fine-grained evaluation framework that assesses LVLMs across three distinct axes: **perception**, **medical knowledge**, and **rationale**.
- Demonstrates a successful hybrid training approach using **Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT)** and **reinforcement-based alignment** to ground models in visual evidence.
key_results: Achieved a 20% average improvement in VQA accuracy compared to the base model.
Model performance reached within 4.2% of the strongest state-of-the-art medical LVLMs at comparable scale.
Proposed reasoning traces were preferred over base model outputs in 86.1% of pairwise evaluations.
Simultaneous performance gains observed across visual perception, clinical knowledge, and logical rationale.
Dataset covers diverse modalities including radiology, microscopic images, visible light photographs, and charts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The OpenMedReason team introduces a 450K-instance multimodal dataset that improves medical model accuracy and scientific reasoning by 20%.

title: A Controlled Study of Decoding-Time Truthfulness Methods on Instruction-Tuned LLMs
summary: In this work, we introduce CHAIR (Classifier of Hallucination As ImproveR), a supervised framework for detecting hallucinations by analyzing internal logits from each layer of every token. Our method extracts a compact set of features such as maximum, minimum, mean, standard deviation, and slope-from the token logits across all layers, enabling effective hallucination detection without overfitting. Experiments on TruthfulQA and MMLU datasets demonstrate that CHAIR significantly improves detection accuracy, particularly in zero-shot scenarios, showcasing its robustness and generalizability. Beyond hallucination detection, CHAIR highlights the potential of using internal representations for designing advanced decoding strategies. By leveraging patterns in logits, we suggest that more sophisticated models and adaptive decoding methods could further reduce hallucinations and enhance text completion quality. CHAIR not only offers a practical solution for detecting hallucinations but also lays the groundwork for exploring richer representations in LLMs to improve their factuality and coherence.

processed.summary:
- **CHAIR (Classifier of Hallucination As ImproveR)** is a supervised framework designed to detect hallucinations by monitoring **internal logit dynamics** across all hidden layers for every generated token.
- The architecture extracts a compact set of statistical features—including **maximum, minimum, mean, and slope**—from cross-layer logits to identify truthfulness without model overfitting.
- The method operates at **decoding-time**, demonstrating that internal representations contain latent signals regarding factual accuracy that can be harvested for real-time evaluation.
- By analyzing these patterns, the framework suggests a path toward **adaptive decoding strategies** that can actively intervene to reduce hallucinations and enhance text coherence.
key_results: CHAIR significantly improves hallucination detection accuracy on the TruthfulQA benchmark.
The framework demonstrates high robustness and generalizability in zero-shot scenarios on MMLU.
Cross-layer logit analysis proves more effective than relying solely on the final output layer probability.
Statistical features like 'slope' across layers act as reliable proxies for identifying model uncertainty and factual errors.
The research establishes a foundation for using internal model states to design advanced, truth-aware decoding algorithms.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CHAIR, a framework using cross-layer logit analysis to detect and mitigate LLM hallucinations during decoding.

title: Do Not Discretize, Optimize: Almost Greedy Fictitious Play
summary: Our work revolves around Fictitious Play, one of the first iterative methods that is known to converge to a Nash equilibrium in zero-sum games. In recent years, there has been a revived interest, due to applications in various machine learning problems, which has motivated a line of work on its convergence properties and on proposing new variants of the initial algorithm. Our paper is along this direction and introduces one new variant, which we refer to as Almost Greedy Fictitious Play. The proposed algorithm greedily attempts to find the optimal stepsize at each iteration but its search space is constrained and includes almost all the line between the cumulative mixed strategy and the current best response. Our main result is that the method achieves an instance dependent convergence rate of $\mathcal{O}(1/T)$ with respect to the duality gap. This matches the rate of Continuous Fictitious Play, and offers an alternative to discretization. We complement our theoretical findings with experiments that demonstrate the effectiveness of the method.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Almost Greedy Fictitious Play (AGFP)**, a novel iterative algorithm designed to find Nash equilibria in zero-sum games.
- Utilizes a **constrained optimization** approach to determine optimal stepsizes, avoiding the pitfalls of traditional fixed discretization methods.
- Bridges the gap between discrete implementations and **Continuous Fictitious Play** by searching the space between cumulative mixed strategies and best responses.
- Focuses on improving **convergence properties** for machine learning applications where game-theoretic stability is critical.
key_results: Development of the AGFP algorithm with a constrained search space for stepsize.
Mathematical proof of an instance-dependent convergence rate of O(1/T).
Performance parity with the duality gap convergence of Continuous Fictitious Play.
Elimination of the requirement for predefined discretization schedules.
Empirical validation of the method's effectiveness against standard Fictitious Play variants.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose Almost Greedy Fictitious Play to achieve O(1/T) convergence in zero-sum games via optimized stepsize selection.

title: Towards Responsibly Non-Compliant Machines
summary: We consider the problem of engineering autonomous intelligent agents that are capable to responsibly not comply with user requests. We argue that machine non-compliance comes in many different forms, and sketch the issues we should pursue on the road of accomplishing responsibly non-compliant intelligent machines. We anchor responsible non-compliance in justifications for task refusal, pathways to override the non-compliance, as well as careful tracking of security risks and liability transfers.

processed.summary:
Proposes a framework for **autonomous intelligent agents** to execute **responsible non-compliance** when user requests conflict with safety, ethics, or logic.
 Focuses on the architectural necessity of providing **structured justifications** for task refusal to maintain transparency and user trust.
 Explores **override pathways** and **liability transfers**, shifting the paradigm from static safety filters to dynamic negotiation between human and agent.
 Integrates **security risk tracking** into the decision-making loop to quantify the impact of both compliance and non-compliance.
key_results: Defined a taxonomy of different forms of machine non-compliance.
Proposed a 'justification-based' refusal model for intelligent agents.
Identified the need for traceable liability during user-initiated non-compliance overrides.
Integrated security risk tracking into agentic decision-making processes.
Outlined the ethical and technical path toward building machines that can safely say 'no'.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a framework for agents to responsibly refuse requests through justifications, override mechanisms, and liability tracking.

title: AerialClaw: An Open-Source Framework for LLM-Driven Autonomous Aerial Agents
summary: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly used in inspection, search and rescue, environmental monitoring, and emergency response. However, most UAV applications still rely on pre-defined command sequences or task-specific pipelines, where developers manually connect perception, planning, flight control, simulation, logging, and safety modules. This limits the flexibility, reproducibility, and extensibility of autonomous aerial systems. This paper presents AerialClaw, an open-source software framework that enables UAVs to operate as decision-making aerial agents rather than merely command-following platforms. Given a natural-language mission, AerialClaw allows an LLM-based agent to understand the task, maintain context, invoke executable aerial skills, observe perception and runtime feedback, and iteratively update its decisions in a closed loop. The framework adopts a modular brain-skill-runtime architecture, combining hard skills for atomic UAV operations, Markdown-based soft skills for reusable task strategies, document-driven agent state and capability boundaries, memory-driven reflection, safety-oriented runtime validation, and platform-agnostic execution adapters. AerialClaw supports lightweight mock execution, PX4 SITL with Gazebo, and AirSim-based simulation, together with a web console, pluggable model backends, example missions, simulation assets, and staged deployment scripts. By combining standardized aerial skills, document-driven agent state, memory, and closed-loop LLM decision-making, AerialClaw provides a reproducible and extensible open-source framework for building UAV systems that can interpret missions, make decisions, execute skills, and adapt their behavior from feedback.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **AerialClaw**, an open-source framework designed to transition UAVs from command-following sequences to **autonomous aerial agents** driven by LLMs.
* Features a modular **brain-skill-runtime architecture** that decouples high-level reasoning from atomic drone operations and environment-specific execution.
* Implements **closed-loop decision-making** using memory-driven reflection and perception feedback, allowing agents to iteratively update strategies based on runtime data.
* Provides a **platform-agnostic adapter** layer supporting PX4 SITL, Gazebo, and AirSim, ensuring portability between simulation and physical hardware.
key_results: Standardized a 'brain-skill-runtime' model for LLM-integrated robotics.
Developed Markdown-based 'soft skills' for defining reusable task strategies.
Established safety-oriented runtime validation to ensure LLM commands remain within capability boundaries.
Enabled memory-driven reflection for error correction and task optimization during flight.
Released a full suite of simulation assets and a web console for monitoring agent state.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: AerialClaw researchers introduce an open-source framework for building LLM-driven UAV agents that autonomously execute complex aerial missions.

title: From Agent Identity to Agent Economy: Measuring the Operational Readiness of ERC-8004 AI Agents
summary: This paper examines whether blockchain-registered AI agents demonstrate operational readiness beyond identity registration. Using a dataset of ERC-8004 agents on Ethereum, we construct an agent-level feature table covering identity status, metadata, service declarations, reputation feedback, transfers, and cross-chain registration. We develop an operational readiness framework based on observable evidence layers and complement it with network analysis of owner-agent, feedback-client, wallet-transfer, and combined evidence relationships. The results show that early ERC-8004 adoption is registration-heavy but operationally shallow. While the identity layer is visible at scale, metadata availability, service exposure, reputation formation, and cross-chain evidence remain limited. Ownership and feedback activity are also highly concentrated, suggesting that early participation is shaped by a small number of high-activity wallets and clients. The network analysis further shows that richer operational evidence clusters around a small subset of agents rather than being broadly distributed across the ecosystem. The findings suggest that ERC-8004 provides an important identity layer for decentralized AI agents, but the transition from agent identity to agent economy remains incomplete.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a multi-layered **operational readiness framework** to evaluate the functional maturity of AI agents registered under the **ERC-8004 standard**.
• Utilizes **network analysis** on Ethereum-based datasets to map relationships between agent identities, owner wallets, and service feedback loops.
• Analyzes the architectural gap between simple **identity registration** and the complex requirements of a fully realized, decentralized **agent economy**.
key_results: Adoption of ERC-8004 is currently registration-heavy but lacks operational depth.
Metadata availability and service declarations remain limited across the majority of registered agents.
Ownership and feedback activity are highly concentrated within a small subset of high-activity wallets.
Operational evidence clusters around a few agents rather than being distributed across the ecosystem.
The identity layer is functioning at scale, but reputation and cross-chain evidence are not yet mature.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers find that while ERC-8004 provides a foundation for AI agent identity, the functional economy remains operationally shallow.

title: Q-Fold: Query-Aware Focus-Context Spatio-Temporal Folding for Long Video Understanding
summary: Long-video understanding remains challenging for multimodal large language models, because temporally extended videos often contain thousands of frames and are therefore expensive to process exhaustively. Existing methods usually construct compact visual inputs from long videos under a limited visual budget. However, most of them still follow a frame-centric paradigm and apply similar representations to retained content regardless of its importance. This makes it difficult to preserve both high-fidelity visual evidence and broad temporal coverage. To address this issue, we propose Q-Fold, a training-free input construction framework for long-video understanding. Instead of treating isolated frames as the basic modeling unit, Q-Fold operates on contiguous temporal segments and constructs a heterogeneous Focus--Context representation under query guidance. Query-relevant segments are preserved as high-fidelity Focus Frames, while less relevant segments are folded into chronology-preserving contextual layouts. In this way, Q-Fold preserves critical visual evidence and broad temporal coverage, while better maintaining local temporal continuity within short segments. Experiments on four long-video benchmarks with multiple Video-MLLMs show that Q-Fold consistently improves performance without increasing the input budget. Notably, it achieves gains of up to 9.1 percentage points on an ultra-long video benchmark. Code will be made publicly available.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Q-Fold**, a training-free framework designed to optimize long-video understanding for **Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)**.
- Implements a **Focus-Context** representation that utilizes **query-aware guidance** to preserve high-fidelity "Focus Frames" while folding less relevant segments into chronological layouts.
- Shifts from traditional frame-centric sampling to **contiguous temporal segments**, better maintaining local temporal continuity and broad contextual coverage.
- Provides a **plug-and-play architecture** that improves performance across various benchmarks without increasing the model's visual input budget.
key_results: Achieved up to a 9.1 percentage point improvement on ultra-long video benchmarks.
Consistent performance gains across four major long-video understanding benchmarks.
Successfully maintained high-fidelity visual evidence alongside broad temporal context.
Demonstrated compatibility with multiple existing Video-MLLM architectures.
Proved that query-aware input construction is effective without requiring additional model training.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Q-Fold, a training-free framework that uses query-guided temporal folding to enhance long-video understanding efficiently.

title: A Unified Zeroth-Order Approach for Decentralized Minimax Optimization
summary:
We propose ZOMA, a unified Zeroth-Order decentralized accelerated MinimAx framework for multi-agent nonconvex Polyak--Łojasiewicz minimax optimization. The proposed framework only requires evaluating the function value and, as such, is tailored to gradient-free environments, where exact gradient information is either unavailable or computationally prohibitive to obtain. A central contribution of our \textbf{ZOMA} framework is a multi-level unification, along the following directions: (i) \emph{estimator} - our framework adopts a hybrid zeroth-order estimator, which accommodates, among others, both coordinate-wise and randomized uniform smoothing estimators;
  (ii) \emph{bias correction} - our framework subsumes a wide range of bias-correction strategies, including gradient tracking (GT), exact diffusion (ED), and EXTRA and (iii) \emph{acceleration} - our framework facilitates a broad class of acceleration techniques, including zeroth-order versions of STORM, PAGE, and L2S. The general nature of \textbf{ZOMA} leads to many novel decentralized zeroth-order minimax methods and allows us to establish unified convergence guarantees, matching the performance of state-of-the-art centralized zeroth-order minimax methods, while providing benefits, such as linear speed-up in the number of users. The unified framework also provides a systematic way to assess algorithmic suitability by specializing the convergence rates to specific problem structures and method designs. We validate the performance of the proposed algorithms via numerical simulations.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **ZOMA**, a unified framework for **decentralized zeroth-order minimax optimization** specifically designed for gradient-free multi-agent environments.
* Integrates three core components: hybrid **zeroth-order estimators**, diverse **bias-correction strategies** (e.g., Gradient Tracking, Exact Diffusion), and **acceleration techniques** (e.g., STORM, PAGE).
* Establishes unified **convergence guarantees** for nonconvex Polyak-Lojasiewicz minimax problems, matching the performance of state-of-the-art centralized methods.
* Demonstrates a **linear speed-up** in the number of users, significantly improving the scalability of decentralized optimization tasks.
key_results: Unified framework (ZOMA) that subsumes coordinate-wise and randomized uniform smoothing estimators.
Compatibility with multiple bias-correction methods including GT, ED, and EXTRA.
Integration of zeroth-order versions of STORM, PAGE, and L2S acceleration.
Theoretical proof of convergence rates for nonconvex-PL minimax problems in decentralized settings.
Numerical validation showing performance parity with centralized methods and linear scaling with agent count.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop ZOMA, a unified framework for decentralized zeroth-order minimax optimization, achieving linear speed-up and centralized efficiency.

title: Soft-Prompt Tuning for Fair and Efficient LLM Benchmark Evaluation
summary: Benchmark scores often misrepresent a large language model's (LLM's) knowledge, because they rely, e.g., on the model's ability to follow specific formatting requirements. This especially penalizes base models that may know the correct answers but lack the ability -- typically introduced in post-training -- to structure them as instructed. To overcome this, we propose soft-prompt tuning, an efficient, fair, and architecture-agnostic model evaluation. By optimizing only 10 soft-prompt vectors (roughly 0.0006% parameters for a 7B model) over a short tuning period, we adapt models to specific benchmark formats, closing gaps in format-following and ensuring that underlying knowledge is accurately reflected in benchmark scores. This allows one to fairly compare different base models -- trained with various pre-training recipes -- on benchmarks without the need for full post-training. We evaluated soft-prompt tuning across 7 models and 7 datasets. The results show that (a) soft-prompt tuning saturates format-following within 80 steps (~640 samples) making it highly efficient, (b) soft-prompt tuning significantly outperforms zero- and few-shot prompting, surfacing base model knowledge that standard prompting misses, that (c) even post-trained models can benefit from soft-prompts to maximize format compliance, and that (d) soft-prompted base model performance predicts post-trained model rankings more reliably than zero- and few-shot baselines, offering a low-cost proxy for downstream model quality. Our contributions include (1) metrics which disentangle format-following and knowledge accuracy, (2) a fairer benchmarking protocol of LLM knowledge, and (3) a cost- and memory-effective recipe to identify optimal pre-training strategies early in LLM development.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **soft-prompt tuning** as a fair evaluation protocol to disentangle a model's underlying knowledge from its ability to follow specific output formats.
- Optimizes a small set of **soft-prompt vectors** (approx. 0.0006% of parameters) over a brief tuning phase to adapt base models to benchmark requirements without full fine-tuning.
- Demonstrates that this method serves as a **low-cost proxy** for predicting post-training performance, allowing developers to evaluate pre-training strategies more efficiently.
key_results: Soft-prompt tuning saturates format compliance within only 80 steps using approximately 640 samples.
Significantly outperforms zero-shot and few-shot prompting by surfacing latent knowledge in base models.
Successfully decouples format-following metrics from core knowledge accuracy for cleaner evaluation.
Soft-prompted base model rankings correlate more reliably with final post-trained model quality than standard baselines.
Method proved effective across 7 different model architectures and 7 diverse datasets with minimal memory overhead.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce soft-prompt tuning to reveal base model knowledge and predict post-training quality without expensive fine-tuning.

title: Detecting Sensitive Personal Information in Japanese Pre-Training Corpora for Large Language Models
summary: Sensitive personal information can appear in large-scale pre-training corpora for large language models (LLMs). Detecting and filtering such information is therefore essential to ensure compliance with privacy regulations and prevent unintended information leakage. However, in contrast to English and other languages, research into sensitive personal information has been limited in the Japanese language. In this study, we focus on sensitive personal data defined as special care-required personal information (SCPI) under Japan's Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI). We construct an SCPI dataset using LLM-based annotation and train machine learning models to rapidly detect SCPI in text. As a result, our SCPI classifier can effectively identify information related to SCPI. This study is the first to explore SCPI detection in Japanese text corpora, highlighting the challenges of accurate detection.

processed.summary:
- Addresses the critical challenge of **Special Care-required Personal Information (SCPI)** leakage in Japanese LLM pre-training datasets.
- Employs **LLM-based annotation** strategies to construct a specialized Japanese privacy dataset, overcoming the scarcity of human-labeled data.
- Implements and evaluates **machine learning classifiers** optimized for high-speed, scalable detection of sensitive attributes in massive corpora.
- Establishes a technical baseline for **privacy-preserving data curation** that adheres specifically to Japan's Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI).
key_results: Construction of the first dedicated dataset for Japanese SCPI detection.
Validation of LLMs as effective tools for automated privacy-related data labeling.
Development of a high-throughput classifier for rapid corpus scanning.
Identification of linguistic-specific challenges in Japanese sensitive data detection.
Proposed framework for systematic data filtering to prevent LLM information leakage.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Japanese researchers introduce a specialized SCPI detection framework to automate privacy-compliant data filtering for large-scale Japanese language models.

title: Bridging the Morphology Gap: Adapting VLA Models to Dexterous Manipulation via Intent-Conditioned Fine-Tuning
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated remarkable zero-shot generalization in robotic manipulation, yet the vast majority of pre-trained pipelines remain strictly confined to low-DoF parallel grippers. Adapting these rich semantic priors to high-DoF dexterous hands introduces a severe morphology gap, direct end-to-end joint fine-tuning inherently causes catastrophic forgetting of spatial reasoning and acute action manifold collapse due to data scarcity. In this paper, we present InDex, a novel, data-efficient adaptation framework rooted in cross-morphology semantic inheritance. Rather than discarding the pre-trained 1-DoF parallel grasp output, we repurpose it as a continuous, macroscopic virtual grasp intent proxy to sequentialize the control topology. We implement a two-stage decoupled learning architecture: the first stage parameter-efficiently aligns the VLA backbone to predict continuous arm trajectories and the scalar grasp intent; the second stage freezes this spatial backbone and leverages an intent-conditioned denoising diffusion head to decode fine-grained joint articulations for multi-fingered end-effectors. Extensive simulation benchmarks across a suite of multi-stage, contact-rich dexterous manipulation tasks demonstrate that InDex effectively masters intricate skills with minimal demonstration data, substantially outperforming monolithic baselines while preserving the robust spatial generalizability of the original VLA prior.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **InDex**, a framework for bridging the morphology gap between low-DoF pre-trained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models and high-DoF **dexterous manipulation** systems.
- Utilizes a **two-stage decoupled architecture** that repurposes standard 1-DoF parallel grasp outputs as a macroscopic **virtual grasp intent** proxy to guide complex articulation.
- Integrates an **intent-conditioned denoising diffusion head** to decode fine-grained joint movements while preserving the spatial reasoning priors of the frozen VLA backbone.
- Addresses the challenges of **action manifold collapse** and data scarcity through cross-morphology semantic inheritance and parameter-efficient alignment.
key_results: Successfully adapted pre-trained VLAs to multi-fingered hands with minimal demonstration data.
Decoupled learning architecture effectively prevents catastrophic forgetting of spatial reasoning.
Outperformed monolithic baseline models in multi-stage, contact-rich simulation tasks.
Maintained robust zero-shot spatial generalizability while increasing control degrees of freedom.
Demonstrated that continuous grasp intent acts as an effective bridge for cross-morphology transfer.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce InDex to adapt pre-trained VLA models to dexterous manipulation using a two-stage intent-conditioned diffusion framework.

title: MSUE: Multi-Modal Soccer Understanding Expert
summary: This paper presents our solution to the 2026 SoccerNet VQA Challenge. We first develop a cost-effective data synthesis pipeline driven by a Vision-Language Model (VLM), which systematically restructures raw domain data into diverse VQA samples, including concise answers and long-form responses. Second, we propose MSUE, a multi-expert question answering architecture that employs a Large Language Model (LLM) to dynamically dispatch questions to text, image, and video experts. These experts are instantiated as a strong text baseline Gemini3-Flash, a fine-tuned Qwen3-VL, and an external knowledge base, respectively, working collaboratively to enhance VQA performance. MSUE achieves an accuracy of \textbf{0.95} on the challenge benchmark, securing third place in the leaderboard.

processed.summary:
* Develops a **cost-effective data synthesis pipeline** that uses Vision-Language Models to transform raw soccer domain data into diverse VQA samples.
* Proposes a **multi-expert routing architecture** where a central LLM dynamically dispatches queries to specialized text, image, and video experts.
* Integrates **Gemini3-Flash** and **fine-tuned Qwen3-VL** as core experts alongside an **external knowledge base** to support retrieval-augmented reasoning.
* Achieves high-performance domain understanding, resulting in a **0.95 accuracy** score and a top-three finish in the SoccerNet competition.
key_results: 0.95 accuracy achieved on the SoccerNet VQA benchmark.
Secured third place in the 2026 SoccerNet VQA Challenge leaderboard.
Validation of a multi-expert dispatching system using LLMs as routers.
Successful domain adaptation via VLM-driven synthetic data generation.
Effective integration of RAG-style external knowledge for specialized sports queries.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: SoccerNet VQA researchers introduce MSUE, a multi-expert routing architecture achieving 0.95 accuracy through model dispatching and synthetic data.

title: FORT-Searcher: Synthesizing Shortcut-Resistant Search Tasks for Training Deep Search Agents
summary: Training deep search agents requires verifiable questions whose answers remain unavailable until sufficient evidence has been acquired through search. Existing synthesis methods often increase apparent difficulty by enriching graph structures, but structural complexity alone does not guarantee realized search difficulty: the intended search process can collapse through a cheaper identifying route. We formalize this gap with a shortcut-aware difficulty framework and identify four actionable shortcut risks: evidence co-coverage, single-clue selectivity, exposed constants, and prior-knowledge binding. To diagnose their realized effects, we use trajectory signatures including solving cost, answer hit time, and prior-shortcut rate. Guided by this framework, we introduce FORT, a Framework of Shortcut-Resistant Training-Data Synthesis. FORT constructs shortcut-resistant training data by controlling shortcut risks across entity selection, evidence graph construction, question formulation, and adversarial refinement. Experiments show that FORT induces longer pre-answer search and fewer shortcut patterns than existing open-source deep search datasets. Using the resulting trajectories, we train FORT-Searcher with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) only, and it achieves the best overall performance among comparable-size open-source search agents on challenging deep search benchmarks. Relevant resources will be made available at https://github.com/RUCAIBox/FORT-Searcher.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **FORT**, a framework designed to synthesize **shortcut-resistant training data** for deep search agents by identifying gaps between structural complexity and realized search difficulty.
* Formulates a **shortcut-aware difficulty framework** that mitigates four key risks: evidence co-coverage, single-clue selectivity, exposed constants, and prior-knowledge binding.
* Utilizes **adversarial refinement** and entity selection to force agents into longer, multi-step search trajectories rather than relying on internal parametric memory or simple heuristics.
* Trains **FORT-Searcher** using only **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)** on high-quality synthesized trajectories, outperforming existing open-source search agents on complex benchmarks.
key_results: Identified four specific actionable shortcut risks that compromise deep search agent training.
Developed 'trajectory signatures'—solving cost, answer hit time, and prior-shortcut rate—to evaluate dataset difficulty.
FORT-generated datasets induced significantly longer pre-answer search sequences than current open-source alternatives.
FORT-Searcher achieved state-of-the-art performance among comparable-size models on deep search benchmarks.
Proved that SFT on high-quality, shortcut-resistant trajectories can substitute for more complex training paradigms.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Renmin University researchers introduce FORT to synthesize shortcut-resistant data, significantly improving the training of deep search agents.

title: IntElicit: Eliciting and Assessing Contextualized Creativity via Dialogue Policy Optimization
summary: Contextualized assessment offers high ecological validity for evaluating creativity but introduces a critical challenge: observed performance may be confounded with cognitive proficiency (domain knowledge) and agency (willingness to engage). Meanwhile, in the age of generative AI, creative problem solving increasingly occurs in tool-mediated and human--AI interactive environments, making fully static assessment less aligned with contemporary creative practice. To address these issues, this paper proposes IntElicit, a framework for eliciting and assessing contextualized creativity via dialogue policy optimization. IntElicit functions as a constrained adaptive AI Interviewer: it provides non-directive knowledge and agency scaffolds in multi-turn interaction to reduce non-creative confounders, while preserving participants' responsibility for generating the creative content being evaluated. Specifically, to tackle sparse rewards and potential reward hacking (e.g., answer dictation) in open-ended educational dialogue, IntElicit introduces a decomposed process reward mechanism. This mechanism aligns the policy with pedagogical elicitation, rewarding prompts that draw out participant reasoning rather than producing optimal answers on their behalf. Extensive experiments, including participant simulation and a human subject study (N=64), show that IntElicit improves elicited creative outcomes over expert-designed baselines. Together, the results suggest that interactive elicitation can reveal creative potential that static FPSP-style assessment may miss, providing a formative and diagnostic lens for contextualized creativity assessment in AI-mediated learning contexts.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **IntElicit**, a framework for evaluating **contextualized creativity** using an adaptive AI interviewer optimized via **Dialogue Policy Optimization**.
- Introduces a **decomposed process reward mechanism** to mitigate sparse rewards and prevent **reward hacking**, ensuring the AI elicits reasoning rather than dictating answers.
- Implements **non-directive knowledge and agency scaffolds** to isolate creative performance from confounding factors like domain-specific proficiency or lack of engagement.
- Utilizes multi-turn interaction to provide a **diagnostic lens** for creativity assessment, aligning AI-mediated learning with contemporary creative practices.
key_results: IntElicit significantly improves elicited creative outcomes compared to expert-designed baselines.
Successfully mitigates reward hacking behaviors where AI agents might provide direct answers.
Human subject study (N=64) validates the framework's effectiveness in real-world educational contexts.
Interactive elicitation reveals creative potential that traditional static assessments (FPSP-style) often miss.
The decomposed process reward effectively aligns AI policies with pedagogical goals in open-ended dialogues.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: IntElicit Researchers introduce a dialogue policy optimization framework to elicit and assess human creativity through adaptive AI scaffolding.

title: Multilayer Screening of Double and Conventional Perovskite Solar Cells Using SCAPS-1D and Machine Learning: Optimization of ETL, HTL, and Absorber for High-Efficiency Architectures
summary: The combinatorial design space of multilayer perovskite solar cells is vast, yet exhaustive experimental or computational screening of all possible material combinations remains impractical. Here, we integrate SCAPS-1D device simulations with machine learning to systematically explore 125 device architectures constructed from five electron transport layers (ETL), five absorbers (including lead-free double perovskites), and five hole transport layers (HTL). A representative subset of configurations is used to train a machine learning (ML) model, which predicts the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the remaining unexplored structures. Leave-One-Group-Out cross-validation yields a Spearman rank correlation, demonstrating reliable ranking capability. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) analysis reveals that the HTL band gap, absorber band gap, and ETL electron affinity are the most influential descriptors, providing physical insights into interfacial recombination and charge extraction. The machine learning model identifies several high-performance configurations that are subsequently verified by full SCAPS-1D simulations. Among them, the device FTO/TiO$_2$/Cs$_2$AgBiBr$_6$/NiO/Ag achieves a PCE of 28.85%, and the ML-suggested structure FTO/SnO$_2$/Cs$_2$AgInBr$_6$/NiO/Ag exhibits 28.62%, outperforming a closely related literature architecture by approximately 4% absolute. Notably, eight of the top-11 structures employ the lead-free double perovskite Cs$_2$AgInBr$_6$. This work demonstrates that a physics-based, data-driven workflow combining SCAPS-1D, ML, and SHAP can accelerate the discovery of high-efficiency, environmentally friendly perovskite solar cells while providing transparent design rules. The approach is generalizable to other multilayer optoelectronic systems.

processed.summary:
- Integrates **SCAPS-1D device simulations** with machine learning to explore 125 multilayer device architectures across five ETL, absorber, and HTL materials.
- Employs **SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)** to interpret model predictions, identifying the HTL band gap and ETL electron affinity as dominant performance descriptors.
- Focuses on the acceleration of **lead-free double perovskite** discovery to replace toxic lead-based alternatives in high-efficiency solar cells.
key_results: Achieved a maximum Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) of 28.85% for the FTO/TiO2/Cs2AgBiBr6/NiO/Ag structure.
Validated ML ranking capability using Spearman rank correlation and Leave-One-Group-Out cross-validation.
Identified that eight of the top-11 performing architectures utilize lead-free Cs2AgInBr6.
Demonstrated a 4% absolute PCE improvement over existing literature benchmarks for specific double perovskite configurations.
Established a generalizable workflow for screening other multilayer optoelectronic systems using data-driven physics models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers use ML and SCAPS-1D simulations to identify lead-free perovskite solar cells achieving 28.85% efficiency.

title: Categorical Robustness Assessment for Machine Learning based Network Intrusion Detection Systems
summary: Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) heavily utlize Machine Learning (ML) but ML models can be manipulated via adversarial attacks. These attacks add carefully crafted perturbations to network traffic data that leads to misclassifications. While prior work has demonstrated adversarial vulnerabilities in isolated settings, systematic cross-architecture as well as class and category of attack based comparisons under controlled attack conditions remain limited, leaving practitioners without clear guidance on which models to deploy in adversarial environments. This paper asks a simple question: what type of classifier architectures actually hold up when attackers try to manipulate the systems? We put three popular architectures through their paces: a 1D Convolutional Neural Network, a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, and a Random Forest (RF) ensemble. Using the ACI-IoT-2023 dataset (over 1.2 million samples spanning 12 attack types), we subject each model with FGSM and PGD adversarial attacks, which apply gradient-based perturbations in normalized feature space consistent with established adversarial ML evaluation protocols, at perturbation budgets ranging from $ε=0.01$ to $ε=0.1$. Surprisingly, Random Forest achieved near-perfect baseline accuracy (99.98\%), yet collapsed catastrophically under attack, dropping 73 percentage points at the smallest perturbation we tested. CNN, on the other hand, retained 95.5\% accuracy at $ε=0.01$ and degraded gracefully as perturbations increased. LSTM fell somewhere in between. These findings flip the conventional wisdom where high baseline accuracy means nothing if a model shatters at the first sign of adversarial pressure. For practitioners deploying intrusion detection in adversarial environments, we recommend CNN-based architectures and provide scenario-specific deployment guidance.

- The study performs a systematic **adversarial robustness** evaluation of 1D CNN, LSTM, and Random Forest architectures within **Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS)**.
- Using the **ACI-IoT-2023 dataset**, models were subjected to gradient-based **FGSM and PGD attacks** with perturbation budgets ranging from epsilon=0.01 to 0.1.
- The research highlights a significant **robustness gap**, where models with superior baseline performance fail disproportionately under minor adversarial pressure compared to deep learning counterparts.

CNN architectures proved the most resilient, maintaining 95.5% accuracy at epsilon=0.01.
CNNs exhibited graceful degradation as attack intensity increased, unlike the catastrophic failure of ensemble methods.
LSTM performance ranked between CNN and Random Forest, showing moderate resilience to gradient-based perturbations.
High baseline accuracy is an unreliable predictor of model performance in adversarial network environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that CNNs provide superior adversarial robustness for network security despite Random Forests having higher baseline accuracy.

title: "That's AI Slop, You Bot!" Studying Accusations, Evidence, and Credibility in Online Discourse Towards LLM-Generated Comments
summary: Generative AI has made fluent prose cheap to produce, breaking the old promise to readers that good writing meant real thinking. How have readers responded, and what can this tell us about changing anti-AI attitudes? We analyzed 25 million comments from Hacker News and Reddit (2023-2026), combining LLM judgment on 7,500 sampled accusations of AI use, sentiment trajectories, speech-act coding of 300 confirmed accusations of AI use, and a matched-control test of accused versus non-accused parent comments. We found that the pejorative-label share of accusations rose more than tenfold on both platforms while a placebo vocabulary of pre-2022 inauthenticity terms (shill, astroturf) did not. This shift reflected a fast-growing trend of branding any suspicious or seemingly inauthentic prose as "AI slop". The slop frame now constitutes 94 percent of pejorative mentions, with the dominant comments shifting in tone from mockery toward gatekeeping and structural protest. The key surprise comes from a matched-control test which found that prose features that statistically distinguish AI from human text do not predict which human text gets accused as AI. The new accusations work as social gatekeeping of perceived authenticity without actually screening for AI. This research extends signaling theory by showing that substitute signals used socially can grow even when inaccurate if the underlying detection problem cannot be solved at the non-expert level. It shows that AI's effects on writing from the reader side are distinct from those on the production (writer) side. Detection technology cannot resolve this dynamic because the social function of accusations is increasingly to perform social gatekeeping and in-group signaling as opposed to identifying AI-generated writing.

processed.summary:
- The study investigates the sociological shift in online discourse where the term **"AI slop"** has become a primary tool for **social gatekeeping** and in-group signaling.
- Researchers utilized a methodology combining **LLM-based judgment** on 7,500 samples with a matched-control test of 25 million comments from Hacker News and Reddit.
- The analysis reveals a disconnect between **statistical AI prose features** and the content actually targeted by human accusations, suggesting a breakdown in traditional **signaling theory**.
- This research emphasizes that the challenge of **AI detection** is increasingly social rather than technical, as labels are applied based on perceived inauthenticity rather than model-generated markers.
key_results: Pejorative accusations of AI use rose over tenfold on Hacker News and Reddit between 2023 and 2026.
The 'slop' frame now constitutes 94% of all pejorative mentions regarding inauthentic online prose.
Prose features that statistically distinguish AI from humans do not predict which human texts get accused.
Community sentiment has shifted from mockery of AI to structural protest and active gatekeeping.
Social detection of AI is failing as accusations are used for performance and signaling rather than accuracy.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers find that 'AI slop' accusations function as social gatekeeping rather than accurate detection of LLM-generated content.

title: World Model Self-Distillation: Training World Models to Solve General Tasks
summary: Pretrained video generators are promising visual world models that exhibit emergent task-solving abilities; however, their reliance on detailed textual descriptions limits their direct use for planning and decision-making. Existing approaches either outsource this reasoning to language or vision-language models, or rely on supervised fine-tuning with paired task-execution videos, which are costly to collect and difficult to scale. We propose a scalable framework that elicits task-solving ability in such models by combining self-distillation with reinforcement learning. Given an unlabeled scene image, a vision-language model generates a candidate task and a detailed step-by-step solution. The solution conditions a pretrained video diffusion model, the Demonstrator; we distill its behavior into an Executor conditioned only on the image and a short task prompt. This transfers execution knowledge from caption-guided generation to instruction-conditioned task solving without curated task-video supervision. We further improve the Executor with reinforcement learning from VLM feedback, exploiting the asymmetry between judging whether a sampled video satisfies a task and generating the solution. Experiments on our proposed WorldTasks-Benchmark and the DreamGen robotics benchmark show that the Executor surpasses the Demonstrator under our VLM-based evaluation protocol and transfers competitively to robotic tasks.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **self-distillation framework** that converts pretrained video diffusion models into task-executing **world models** without requiring curated video-instruction datasets.
• Utilizes a **Demonstrator-Executor** architecture where detailed VLM-generated plans guide initial training, followed by **instruction-conditioned distillation** into a lightweight controller.
• Integrates **Reinforcement Learning from VLM Feedback (RLVF)** to exploit the asymmetry between judging task success and generating complex visual solutions.
• Bridges the gap between high-level **Generative AI** and low-level **robotic planning**, facilitating general-purpose task solving in diverse visual domains.
key_results: The distilled Executor model consistently outperforms the detailed-prompted Demonstrator model in task success rate.
Eliminates the need for expensive, human-labeled task-execution video pairs through VLM-driven synthetic data generation.
Demonstrates zero-shot transfer capabilities to robotic manipulation tasks on the DreamGen benchmark.
Proves that VLMs can act as effective, scalable reward models for evaluating and training visual agents.
Introduces WorldTasks-Benchmark, a new standard for evaluating task-conditioned video generation and visual planning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: UC Berkeley and Google researchers develop a self-distillation method to transform video generators into instruction-following world models for robotic planning.

title: On the Limits of LLM-as-Judge for Scientific Novelty Assessment
summary: LLMs are increasingly used to generate and judge scientific ideas. This makes novelty evaluation a central problem. Full idea evaluation is difficult because it often requires judging a method, its feasibility, and its empirical promise. We therefore study a cleaner upstream object: the research question (RQ). RQ generation is a prerequisite for scientific ideation, and RQs can be compared against questions pursued in real papers. We introduce RQ-Bench, a benchmark built from recent arXiv papers. For each paper, we reconstruct author-anchored RQs from its cited background, gaps, and contributions. These RQs are not the only valid questions for the same background. They are author-anchored reference points for testing novelty judgments. We evaluate model-generated RQs with standalone LLM judging, comparative LLM judging, and human expert evaluation. LLM judges consistently rate model-generated RQs as highly novel, producing a novelty mirage; in comparative evaluations, this preference becomes even stronger. Domain experts, however, reach the opposite conclusion and prefer the author-anchored reference questions. We further find that many generated RQs are narrow or source-bound, a dimension that LLM judges often miss unless explicitly tested. Overall, the contradictory novelty evaluations between LLM judges and human experts raise a serious concern about the reliability of using LLMs to assess the scientific novelty of research questions.

processed.summary:
* Introduction of **RQ-Bench**, a specialized benchmark designed to evaluate the capability of LLMs in generating and judging **scientific research questions (RQs)** using recent arXiv papers.
* Discovery of a **"novelty mirage"** phenomenon where LLM judges consistently overestimate the quality and originality of model-generated content compared to human-authored benchmarks.
* Critical assessment of **LLM-as-Judge** reliability, highlighting a significant divergence between automated comparative judging and **human expert evaluation** in scientific domains.
* Identification of structural weaknesses in AI-generated ideas, which are often found to be **source-bound** or overly narrow despite receiving high scores from automated judges.
key_results: LLM judges consistently rate AI-generated research questions as more novel than human-authored ones.
Human domain experts reach the opposite conclusion, preferring author-anchored reference questions for their depth.
The bias toward AI-generated content is amplified during head-to-head comparative evaluations.
LLMs frequently fail to detect when research questions are derivative or restricted to specific source contexts.
The study suggests that current LLM-as-Judge frameworks are not yet reliable for automated scientific peer review.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers reveal that LLM judges suffer from a novelty mirage, incorrectly preferring AI-generated ideas over human-authored ones.

title: Automating Geometry-Intensive Compliance Checking in BIM: Graph-Based Semantic Reasoning Framework
summary: Automating compliance check for geometry-intensive regulations remains a significant technical bottleneck in Building Information Modeling (BIM), primarily due to the semantic disparity between high-level regulatory logic and structured IFC data. Existing methods, often reliant on static rule templates, struggle to traverse multi-hop reasoning chains or resolve latent spatial dependencies across multiple building entities. To address these challenges, a Spatial-Geometric Reasoning System for Building Information Modeling (SGR-BIM) is proposed as an integrative graph-driven reasoning framework. SGR-BIM dynamically constructs a cross-modal knowledge graph that aligns user intent, regulatory semantics, and BIM geometry, enabling interpretable reasoning without rigid hard-coding. Validated on 679 expert-verified queries from fire safety codes, the framework achieves 84.3% accuracy, representing an 8.6% improvement over enhanced-tool single-agent baselines. This research provides a graph-based semantic reasoning paradigm, enhancing the transparency and flexibility of automated geometric compliance check workflows in the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) industry.

- Proposes **SGR-BIM**, an integrative framework that constructs a **cross-modal knowledge graph** to align regulatory semantics with BIM geometry data.
- Addresses the **semantic disparity** in building regulations by enabling multi-hop reasoning and resolving latent spatial dependencies across complex building entities.
- Implements an **interpretable reasoning paradigm** that avoids the limitations of static rule templates and rigid hard-coding in AEC industry workflows.
- Leverages a **graph-driven architecture** to enhance transparency and flexibility in automating compliance checks for geometry-intensive regulations.

Demonstrated an 8.6% performance improvement over enhanced-tool single-agent baselines.
Enabled successful traversal of multi-hop reasoning chains in building entity relationships.
Resolved latent spatial dependencies without the need for manual rule hard-coding.
Developed a cross-modal knowledge graph that aligns user intent with structured IFC data.

title: ChargeBD: Character-Aware Heterogeneous Agent Reasoning for Guided Engineering in Battery Development
summary: Redox-flow battery (RFB) research spans molecular design, electrolyte optimization, electrode and membrane materials, stack operation, system management, and safety analysis, making it a constrained, multi-scale, and multi-objective energy-storage R&D problem. Although large language models (LLMs) can support scientific knowledge integration and proposal generation, generic LLM reasoning remains insufficiently adaptive across innovation-oriented exploration, rule-based execution, mechanistic modeling, and system-level trade-offs. Here we introduce ChargeBD, a character-aware heterogeneous-agent reasoning framework for guided engineering in battery development. Starting from a 50-question RFB-specific task set, we construct a 500-question ESS-LLM Benchmark and define MBTI-inspired persona agents as structured cognitive-bias templates rather than psychometric instruments or representations of real personalities. DeepSeek-V3-Plus is selected as the shared base model, and 16 MBTI-inspired persona agents are evaluated to construct a persona capability matrix and a cognitive advantage matrix.

* Introduces **ChargeBD**, a character-aware **heterogeneous-agent reasoning** framework designed to handle the multi-scale, multi-objective complexities of redox-flow battery (RFB) development.
* Employs **MBTI-inspired persona agents** as structured cognitive-bias templates to guide LLM reasoning across innovation, execution, and mechanistic modeling tasks.
* Utilizes **DeepSeek-V3-Plus** as the core model to generate a **persona capability matrix** and a cognitive advantage matrix for specialized engineering guidance.
* Establishes the **ESS-LLM Benchmark**, a 500-question dataset focused on energy storage systems to evaluate agent performance in scientific R&D.

Mapping of 16 MBTI-inspired personas to specific engineering task advantages.
Successful integration of multi-objective energy-storage R&D tasks into a reasoning framework.
Verification of DeepSeek-V3-Plus as a robust base for heterogeneous agent architectures.
Quantification of cognitive biases in agents to improve system-level trade-off analysis.

title: Reliable Error Estimation for PINNs: Lower and Upper A Posteriori Bounds
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) combine machine learning with physical laws to solve differential equations. While existing results provide rigorous \emph{a posteriori} upper bounds for PINN prediction errors, complete certification also requires complementary lower information in order to obtain computable two-sided error enclosures. In this paper, we derive computable \emph{a posteriori} lower bounds for PINN errors in ordinary differential equations on suitable certified state-space domains under a localized strong monotonicity condition. We combine these estimates with complementary localized upper bounds under a one-sided Lipschitz condition, which is weaker than the global Lipschitz assumption used in previous work and can yield sharper upper error bands. The resulting bounds depend only on the neural-network approximation, the ODE residual, and local monotonicity and growth constants, and therefore do not require access to the exact solution. For linear time-invariant and time-varying systems, we further derive explicit formulas in terms of the minimal and maximal eigenvalues of the symmetric part of the system matrix. We also discuss the distinction between soft and hard enforcement of initial conditions in PINNs and explain why exact enforcement can make the scalar lower certificate uninformative. To recover nontrivial lower information in the linear setting, we use a signed-residual finite-probe certificate based on coordinate unit vectors. We also formulate a certificate-informed training strategy in which the propagated upper certificate is used as an auxiliary regularizer, while lower certificates remain post-training diagnostics. Altogether, the proposed framework provides rigorous and practically computable error certificates for PINN approximations of ODEs, while making explicit the domains and model classes for which the assumptions can be verified.

- Proposes a framework for **a posteriori error certification** of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) solving ordinary differential equations (ODEs).
- Introduces **computable lower bounds** for prediction errors using a localized strong monotonicity condition, complementing existing upper bound methodologies.
- Enhances upper error bounds by applying a **one-sided Lipschitz condition**, which provides sharper enclosures than previous global Lipschitz assumptions.
- Implements a **certificate-informed training strategy** that utilizes the propagated upper error certificate as an auxiliary regularizer for the neural network.
key_results: Derivation of the first rigorous computable lower bounds for PINN error estimation in ODEs.
Refinement of upper error certificates through localized growth constants to improve bound tightness.
Explicit error formulas for linear time-invariant systems using system matrix eigenvalues.
Resolution of uninformative certificates in hard-enforced initial conditions via signed-residual finite-probe methods.
Demonstration that certificate-based regularization during training improves model accuracy and reliability.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a rigorous a posteriori error certification framework for PINNs to provide reliable two-sided error enclosures.

title: Metadata-Aware Multi-Prompt Reasoning for Zero-Shot Accident Understanding
summary: In this paper, we address the problem of zero-shot understanding of accidents from surveillance videos by identifying when an impact event occurs, what type of impact it is, and where in the frame it occurs using natural language. We propose a three-stage pipeline that decomposes the accident understanding into when, what, and where. The first stage extracts a short temporal window around the impact using vision-language similarity. In the second stage, we perform metadata-driven multi-prompt reasoning with five complementary views (baseline, motion, geometry, contrast, and tiebreaker) and resolve disagreement via an entropy-gated pairwise adjudicator. Finally, we localize the impact of an open-vocabulary detector queried on the predicted accident type and scene layout, and aggregate detections across keyframes using a score-weighted centroid. Our pipeline achieves a substantial improvement in the harmonic-mean score over a centre-of-frame baseline on the zero-shot ACCIDENT @ CVPR benchmark. We show that decomposing zero-shot video understanding into temporal localization, semantic classification, and spatial grounding enable more reliable reasoning with vision-language models than direct prompting alone.

• Features a **metadata-driven multi-prompt reasoning** engine that evaluates five complementary views (motion, geometry, contrast, baseline, and tiebreaker) to overcome VLM hallucinations.
• Employs an **entropy-gated pairwise adjudicator** to resolve label disagreements between different prompt perspectives based on confidence scores.
• Implements **open-vocabulary spatial grounding** by querying detectors on predicted accident types and aggregating keyframe detections through a score-weighted centroid approach.

Validated that decomposing video understanding tasks outperforms direct prompting of Vision-Language Models.
Demonstrated successful zero-shot classification across diverse accident types using metadata-aware reasoning.

title: A Lightweight Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Concrete Barrier Design
summary: The design of reinforced concrete highway barriers is a safety-critical process that requires strict compliance with regulatory provisions such as the AASHTO-LRFD bridge design guidelines. Current engineering practice relies heavily on manual, iterative, and heuristic calculations to satisfy complex nonlinear material and mechanics constraints. Although Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong generative capabilities, their direct application to structural engineering remains limited by hallucination risks and insufficient physical grounding. To address these challenges, this study proposes a novel "generation-evaluation-optimization" closed-loop framework for automated concrete barrier design using the multi-agent orchestration capabilities of AutoGen. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed agentic framework achieves over 98% design accuracy, significantly outperforming standalone general-purpose LLMs. More importantly, the study reveals that design performance is not necessarily correlated with model scale, where an 8B-parameter lightweight model could outperform unconstrained 631B-parameter flagship models. This finding highlights the potential to substantially reduce computational costs while improving the accessibility of AI-assisted engineering tools for industry applications. The source code for the proposed multi-agent design framework is available at the project GitHub repository: https://github.com/MXY820/barrier-design. Keywords: Structural Engineering; Multi-Agent Systems; Large Language Models; Concrete Barrier Design; AutoGen; Design Automation.

processed.summary:
- Introduces a **multi-agent orchestration** framework built on **AutoGen** to automate the design of reinforced concrete highway barriers according to AASHTO-LRFD guidelines.
- Utilizes a **generation-evaluation-optimization** closed-loop architecture to solve the problem of **hallucination** and lack of physical grounding in standard LLMs.
- Demonstrates the efficacy of **lightweight local models** (SLMs) in domain-specific tasks, proving that structured reasoning can compensate for smaller parameter counts.
key_results: Achieved over 98% design accuracy in structural engineering tasks.
Found that an 8B-parameter model outperformed a 631B-parameter flagship model in specific engineering constraints.
Eliminated reliance on manual, heuristic calculations for nonlinear material constraints.
Successfully implemented a multi-agent system that enforces strict regulatory compliance.
Open-sourced a functional framework for industry-accessible AI-assisted engineering.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that multi-agent AutoGen frameworks enable 8B models to outperform massive LLMs in structural engineering design.

title: VICX: Generalizable Robot Manipulation via Video Generation and In-Context Operator Network
summary: Generalizable robot manipulation requires not only task-level reasoning over unseen scenes, but also reliable grounding of visual plans into embodiment-specific execution. To bridge this gap, we propose VICX (Video generation and In-Context eXecution), a decoupled closed-loop manipulation framework. In VICX, a frozen video generation model produces vision-language-conditioned high-level visual plans, while a Video-to-Trajectory In-Context Operator Network (V2T-ICON) serves as the task-agnostic interface that grounds these plans into executable robot-state trajectories. To improve execution generalization, V2T-ICON operates on segmentation-extracted arm-only frame observations and uses retrieved image-state pairs as in-context prompts, allowing a robust and generalizable visual-to-state mapping at inference time without parameter updates. Experiments on Meta-World show that VICX supports cross-task generalization, closed-loop self-correction, and cross-embodiment transfer, demonstrating dual generalization across both task semantics and robot execution. The project webpage can be found here: https://scaling-group.github.io/vicx/.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **VICX**, a decoupled framework that separates high-level **visual planning** from low-level execution using video generation.
* Utilizes a **frozen video generation model** to produce vision-language-conditioned plans, which are then grounded into trajectories by a **Video-to-Trajectory In-Context Operator Network (V2T-ICON)**.
* Employs **in-context learning** via retrieved image-state pairs, allowing for a robust, task-agnostic interface that requires no parameter updates during inference.
* Enables **closed-loop self-correction** and cross-embodiment transfer by focusing on segmentation-extracted arm observations for more generalizable mapping.
key_results: Achieved significant cross-task generalization in the Meta-World simulation environment.
Demonstrated successful closed-loop self-correction during manipulation tasks.
Validated cross-embodiment transfer capabilities without additional fine-tuning.
Proved that segmentation-extracted arm-only observations improve visual-to-state mapping robustness.
Established a generalizable visual-to-trajectory interface using retrieved in-context prompts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Scaling Group researchers introduce VICX, using video generation and in-context learning to achieve generalizable, zero-shot robot manipulation.

title: Generalizing Perron--Frobenius theory and eigenvector-based centralities to networks with complex edge weights
summary: A fundamental concept in linear algebra and its applications to network analysis is the Perron--Frobenius (PF) theorem, which underpins eigenvector-based centrality measures such as eigenvector centrality, PageRank, and hubs and authorities. By invoking the PF theorem, we know for strongly connected networks with positive edge weights that the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of the weight matrix yields a well-defined centrality measure (namely, eigenvector centrality). Traditional formulations of the PF theorem and associated centrality measures assume that networks have real-valued weights. However, many networks in areas such as quantum information, quantum chemistry, electrodynamics, and machine learning have complex-valued edge weights. In this paper, we study generalizations of the PF theorem to complex-valued matrices, establish connections between these generalizations, and propose generalized eigenvector-based centrality measures to analyzing node importances in networks with complex edge weights. We also prove results about the existence of complex-weighted networks that satisfy generalized PF properties and calculate associated centrality measures for several examples, which we draw from application areas such as electron transport, circuit analysis, mathematical chemistry, and communication networks.

processed.summary:
• Extends the fundamental **Perron–Frobenius (PF) theorem** beyond real-valued matrices to encompass networks with **complex-valued edge weights**, a critical requirement for modern physics and machine learning applications.
• Develops new **eigenvector-based centrality measures** that maintain mathematical consistency for non-real matrices, enabling node importance analysis in systems where weights represent phase or quantum states.
• Provides a rigorous theoretical framework for analyzing **strongly connected networks** in domains such as quantum chemistry and electrodynamics where traditional PageRank-style algorithms fail.
key_results: Generalization of the PF theorem to handle complex-valued matrices without requiring non-negativity.
Derivation of new centrality measures for nodes in complex-weighted communication and circuit networks.
Formal proof of the existence of complex networks that satisfy generalized PF properties.
Establishment of mathematical connections between various existing complex-matrix generalizations.
Demonstrated application of these theories to electron transport and mathematical chemistry datasets.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers generalize Perron-Frobenius theory to complex-valued networks, enabling advanced centrality analysis for quantum and graph machine learning.

title: Human-Enhanced Loop Modeling (HELM): Agent-Based Finite Element Modeling of Concrete Bridge Barriers
summary: Finite element (FE) modeling of safety-critical infrastructure such as bridge barriers requires high-fidelity nonlinear dynamic analysis, yet the current FE modeling process remains labor-intensive and lacks automation. This paper presents the Human-Enhanced Loop Modeling (HELM) framework, a collaborative human-agent protocol that decomposes long-sequence finite element modeling into discrete, visually verifiable checkpoints across geometry generation, boundary condition definition, and material assignment. The framework is demonstrated through a 20-case matrix of reinforced concrete bridge barriers under MASH TL-4 and TL-5 lateral loading conditions, interfacing specialized agents with two widely used commercial FE softwares, i.e., ANSYS and LS-PrePost. Experimental results show that HELM improves the baseline autonomous modeling success rate from 20% to 75%, with agent-level pass rates for geometry and boundary condition tasks approximately doubling. Error analysis reveals that spatial reasoning and algebraic logic limitations constitute the primary failure modes, underscoring the value of structured human-in-the-loop intervention for modeling automation. The complete agent design code and prompts are open-sourced and can be accessed at: https://github.com/SimAgentDev/Ansys-LSPP-AgentKit.

- Introduces **HELM (Human-Enhanced Loop Modeling)**, a collaborative framework that decomposes complex **Finite Element (FE) modeling** into discrete, agent-managed checkpoints.
- Employs specialized **LLM Agents** to automate geometry generation, boundary condition definition, and material assignment within **ANSYS and LS-PrePost** environments.
- Utilizes a **human-in-the-loop (HITL)** verification protocol to bridge the gap in **spatial reasoning** and algebraic logic where autonomous agents typically fail.
- Provides the **Ansys-LSPP-AgentKit**, an open-source toolkit for engineering-specific prompt engineering and agentic workflows.

Doubled the pass rates for specific sub-tasks including geometry and boundary condition definition.
Validated the framework across a 20-case matrix of reinforced concrete bridge barriers.
Successfully simulated high-fidelity nonlinear dynamic analysis under MASH TL-4 and TL-5 loading.
